message.content: Hardware Additions involve adversaries introducing new hardware components into a system or network to gain unauthorized access or enhance their capabilities. Unlike simple payload distribution via removable media, these additions can provide advanced functionalities such as passive network tapping, network traffic modification, keystroke injection, and kernel memory reading via Direct Memory Access (DMA). They can also introduce new wireless access points to an existing network. Although public instances of threat actor usage are limited, red teams and penetration testers frequently employ these methods for initial access. Notable examples include the use of devices like Bash Bunny, Raspberry Pi, and inexpensive laptops to infiltrate networks. Mitigation strategies include strict physical security controls, regular hardware audits, network segmentation, and monitoring for unauthorized devices. Implementing robust access controls and employing network intrusion detection systems can also help identify and prevent such hardware-based attacks.
